narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 52.8% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 31.50, Year 1 grade decile of 3, Year 3 decile of 2, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27, undergraduate GPA is 3.24, Year 1 decile is 6.04, and Year 3 decile is 5.91, placing several of your metrics below those benchmarks. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 grade decile, your undergraduate GPA, and your Year 1 grade decile. Your Year 3 decile had the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure, as it signals lower relative performance compared to peers who succeeded. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success by being reasonably competitive, though still slightly below the passing average. Your Year 1 decile also contributed toward a failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about early academic standing. Your middle family income aligns with about 35% of bar passers, and your full-time status matches the vast majority at 93%, so those played smaller roles overall. While your background as an Asian female pursuing full-time studies didn't factor into the model's core decision, the academic indicators dominated. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and focusing on grade improvement strategies that could elevate your deciles in future evaluations. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these areas and reapplying after demonstrable progress. You're clearly motivated, and with targeted improvements, a stronger prediction—and admission—could be within reach.
